nodes.label: tasks
nodes.summary: This project creates a **command** named `tasks` (or `bashes`) that allows users to view and manage background processes. Instead of loading everything at once, it uses a **lazy loading** strategy to fetch the necessary code only when requested, seamlessly launching an interactive **React-based interface** to handle the user's actions.

children.label: Command Definition & Registration
children.summary:
This abstraction defines the identity of a feature within the system. It acts like a "business card" or a menu entry for the command. It tells the main application what the command is called (e.g., "tasks"), what aliases it accepts (e.g., "bashes"), and provides a human-readable description.

Think of it like registering a new app on your phone's home screen; the system needs to know the app's name and icon before it can launch it. This file sets up that metadata so the system recognizes the command exists.

children.label: Lazy Loading Architecture
children.summary:
This pattern allows the application to load the heavy logic of a command only when the user actually asks for it, rather than loading everything at startup.

Imagine a library where books are kept in a separate warehouse. Instead of keeping every single book on the front desk (which would clutter the space), the librarian only fetches a specific book when you request it. This makes the initial startup of the application much faster and consumes less memory until the specific "tasks" feature is needed.

children.label: React-based Command Handler
children.summary:
This represents the bridge between a command-line style trigger and a graphical user interface (GUI). Instead of executing a script that outputs text to a terminal, this handler initializes a React component environment.

It acts like a TV remote button that, instead of just changing a number on a display, turns on a full graphical menu on the screen. The `call` function is the specific signal that says, "Stop processing text, and start rendering this interactive window."

children.label: UI Component Delegation
children.summary:
This abstraction separates the *logic of launching* the command from the *visual presentation* of the command. The entry file doesn't draw the UI itself; it immediately hands off responsibility to a specialized component (the `BackgroundTasksDialog`).

It is similar to a receptionist at a doctor's office. The receptionist (the `call` function) greets you and checks you in, but immediately escorts you to the doctor (the `BackgroundTasksDialog`) for the actual procedure. This keeps the entry point clean and focused solely on initialization.

children.label: Context & Lifecycle Injection
children.summary:
This abstraction manages the flow of data and control between the main application and the specific tool. It passes down essential objects like `context` (data needed to run) and `onDone` (a way to signal completion).

Think of this as a manager assigning a job to a contractor. The manager gives the contractor a blueprint (`context`) and a radio (`onDone`) to call in when the job is finished. This ensures the isolated task knows what to do and how to tell the main system it is finished without being tightly coupled.
